questions.q: The hydrogen bomb is based on the principle of?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Nuclear fission and nuclear fusion both are nuclear phenomena that release large amounts of energy, but they are different processes. Nuclear Fusion reactions powers the Sun and other stars. In a fusion reaction, two light nuclei fused together to form a single heavier nucleus. This process releases energy because the total mass of the resulting single nucleus is less than the mass of the two original nuclei. The left over mass becomes energy, as per the Einstein equation of E=mc², m is mass, and c is the speed of light. Hydrogen bomb is based on uncontrolled nuclear fusion. Nuclear fission takes place when an atom's nucleus splits into two or more smaller nuclei. These smaller nuclei are called fission products. It may occur naturally, as in the decay of radioactive isotopes, or it can be forced to occur in a reactor or weapon. Atom bomb is an example of uncontrolled fission reaction. Fission occurs when a neutron slams into a larger atom, forcing it to excite and split into two smaller atoms, also known as fission products. Additional neutrons are also released that can initiate a chain reaction. When each atom splits, a tremendous amount of energy is released. Uranium and plutonium are most commonly used for fission reactions in nuclear power reactors.

No. 2 Info Bits: Superconductivity: It is a phenomenon of exactly zero electrical resistance and expulsion of magnetic flux fields occurring in certain materials called superconductors, when cooled below a characteristic critical temperature. The temperature at which resistance of a matter suddenly becomes zero is known as 'Transition Temperature'. The metals or metallic materials showing the property of super- conductivity are, Mercury (at 4.2 K), Tungsten (at 0.01 K), Cadmium (at 0.56 K), Aluminium(at 1.19 K), Stanus [Tin]( at 3.7 K), Lead (at 7.2 K) etc. During its discovery in 1911 by Dutch physicist Heike Onnes, Superconductivity was thought to exist only at temperatures close to absolute zero (-273.15°C), but recently it has been established that Superconductivity exists at higher temperatures also. Lanthanum hydride (LaH₁₀) becomes a super-conductor at around 250-260 K A superconductor made from Hydrogen, Carbon and Sulphur (Carbonaceous Sulphur hydride) exists at near room-temperature (around 15°C or 288 K), which is currently the highest temperature at which any material has shown superconductivity. Application of Superconductivity: Powerful superconducting electromagnet used in maglev trains. (Magnetic Levitation' trains work on the principle of magnetic repulsion) Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) machines. Magnetic confinement fusion reactors (e.g. tokamaks). Beam steering and focusing magnets used in particle accelerators. Low loss of power cables.

questions.q: A fan produces a feeling of comfort during the hot weather because

options.B: Our body radiates more heat into the air.
options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: The fast-moving air from the fan increases the rate at which our bodies lose heat due to convection and evaporation. It displaces the warmer air that is in direct contact with our skin. This enhances the rate of convective heat transfer, which means we feel cooler. Moisture in the form of sweat on our skin also evaporates more quickly in the presence of fast-moving air. This takes away some body heat and makes us feel cooler. Info Bits: Sweat consists of water, urea, lactic acid, and salt (NaCl).

questions.explanation: Study of production and properties of the ionic state of matter in physics is called plasma physics. Study of structure of atoms, subatomic energy states and the interaction between atoms is called atomic physics. Study of atomic nuclei, their constituents and interactions are called nuclear physics.

questions.q: The branch of physics that deals with the motion of a very small particle is called
questions.explanation: Quantum Mechanics is the branch of physics that deals with the motion of a very small particle. When we talk about scope of physics, there are mainly two domains: - Macroscopic: It includes phenomena at the laboratory, terrestrial and astronomical scales. Classical physics deals mainly with this phenomenon. Classical physics includes subjects like mechanics, electrodynamics, optics, and thermodynamics. - Microscopic: It includes atomic, molecules and nuclear phenomenon. It basically deals with the constitution and structure of matter at the minute scales of atoms and nuclei and their interaction with different particles such as electrons, photons and other elementary particles. Quantum mechanics is accepted as the proper framework for explaining microscopic phenomena. Hence, option c is the correct answer. Info Bits: Field theory: It can be any theory in physics having a detailed mathematical description of the assumed physical properties of a region under some influence or force such as gravitation. Particle Physics: It is one of the main branches of fundamental physics which deals with the study of elementary constituents of matter and radiation and the interaction between them. Atomic Physics: It is the scientific study of the structure of the atom, its energy states and its interactions with other particles and with electric and magnetic fields. The foundations of atomic physics were laid by the gold foil experiments of Rutherford in May 1911.

questions.q: When switch of an electric appliance is off, it disconnects

options.E: None or more than one.

questions.explanation: On switching off the main switch of an electric appliance, live wire is disconnected in order to break the circuit. Info Bits: A switch or a fuse in an electrical circuit is always connected to the live wire so that the socket or appliance is not live when switched off. In domestic electrical circuits, there are 3 wires - live, earth and neutral - Live wire: The live wire is also called the phase wire. The live wire is connected directly to the generators of the current supply. The main fuse is connected to the live wire. If a fault occurs where the live wire is connected then the Earth wire allows a large current to flow through the live and Earth wires. This overheats the fuse which melts and breaks the circuit. - Neutral wire: The neutral wire is at approximately 0V. The main switch is connected to the live and neutral wires. - Earth wire: The Earth wire usually carries no current. It is a safety wire and connects the metal case of the appliance to the Earth. Earth wire is generally green in colour while the live wire is generally red and the neutral wire is black.

questions.q: A device that is used to save an electric circuit is called
questions.explanation: A fuse is an electric device made up of wires with low melting point and high resistivity that is used to protect the electric appliance or electric circuit from the damage caused by flow of excessive amounts of current through the circuit. It is an electric safety device based on the principle of heating effect of electric current. Most fuse wires are made of alloy of tin and lead. H=I²RT A fuse is installed in the electrical circuit, and when an excessive amount of current starts flowing (either due to short circuit or overload) in the circuit, the wires of the fuse get overheated as the temperature of the wire increases. Once the temperature reaches its melting point the fuse wire melts and the connection of the circuit is disrupted. The circuit gets open thus the excess current did not flow through the circuit and did not cause any damage to the connections or any electrical appliance.

questions.q: What type of lens is used in magnifying glass?

options.E: None of the above/ more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Convex lens is used as a magnifying lens. Info Bits: Convex lens is a lens in which light rays travelling parallel to its primary axis converge towards the principal axis. That is why it is also known as a converging lens because it converges a parallel beam of light on point called the principal focus. The power of a convex lens is always positive. Magnifying glass (lens) is used to produce an enlarged image of an object. It is used in the microscope and to correct Hypermetropia or long-sightedness. Convex mirrors are used in vigilance mirrors and rear-view side mirror as they cover a wider area of view. It is also named as Diverging mirrors because when rays are reflected, they diverge. As a result, when a beam of light is aimed at it, the initial parallel beam of rays diverge after contacting the reflective surface of the mirror, which gives a better view of the backside. Concave Lens: A concave lens is a lens that diverges a straight light beam from the source to a diminished, upright, virtual image. It can form virtual images. Concave lens is used to cure Myopia or short-sightedness, the power of a concave lens is always negative. Concave lens also known as diverging lens as it diverges the rays. Concave mirrors are also known as converging mirrors because they converge light to one prime focus point. A magnified or virtual image is formed when the object is placed very close to the concave mirror. Concave mirrors are primarily used in torches, in examination of patient by Dentists, vehicle headlights, flashlights, and searchlights, shaving mirrors, solar furnaces, etc. Note: Two plane mirrors are placed parallel to each other in Barber's Shop so that multiple images arise from multiple reflections and many reflected images can be seen and one can get a better view of hair from the front and back. The image formed in a plane mirror is an example of virtual image. The size of the image formed in the plane mirror is just equal to the size of the object. The image and objects are at the same distance from the mirror at opposite sides. The image is a laterally inverted mirror image of the object. If a person is reflected in a plane mirror, the image of his right hand appears to be the left hand of the image. Power of the lens is defined as the reciprocal of focal length (f) in meters (m). P= 1/f (m) The SI unit of power of a lens is Diopter. (1D = 1m⁻¹)

questions.q: Large numbers of thin strips of black paint are made on the surface of convex lens of focal length 20 cm to catch the image of white horse. The image will be

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: If a large number of thin strips of black paint are made on the surface of convex lens of focal length 20 cm to catch the image of a white horse the complete image will be formed of a horse because only two rays of light are required to pass through the lens to form an image of a point on the object. Image formation does not depend upon the size of the lens. Since the whole lens is not painted or covered, two rays from each point on the horse will definitely pass through the lens and will form the complete image of the horse but with a reduced intensity or brightness. The intensity of the image will reduce because the total number of rays now passing through the lens will decrease as the overall area through which the refraction was taking place earlier (i.e. before painting) will reduce as the light rays will not pass from the strips of black paint. Hence, a complete image of a horse with less brightness will be formed.

questions.q: The speed of light will be minimum while passing through

options.E: None of the above /more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Speed of light in a medium depends on its Refractive Index (μ), which is the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in that medium. It is also called optical density. Higher the value of the refractive Index of a medium, the lower will be the speed of light in that medium. The Refractive Index is a dimensionless quantity. Of all the four options glass has the maximum Refractive Index of 1.52; hence the speed of light will be minimum in glass. (μ_glass = 1.52 > μ_water = 1.33 > μ_vacuum = 1) Speed of light is highest in a vacuum, while it is lowest in a diamond. Since the light speed is maximum in vacuum, so the value of the refractive index of any medium is always more than 1 in relation to vacuum.

Violet has the highest frequency, lowest speed, and maximum deviation. As we know wavelength (λ) is inversely proportional to the frequency (v), so the wavelength of red light is more than violet light.

questions.q: When a soap film on the water is seen in the daytime, it shows beautiful colours. This phenomenon is due to

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: The beautiful colours seen on the soap film are due to interference patterns, created when light reflects off the two surfaces of the thin soap film. Interference patterns are created when two reflected waves line up in phase or out of phase. Refraction of light caused by the earth's atmosphere due to varying optical densities of different layers of the earth's atmosphere is called atmospheric refraction. Some examples of atmospheric refraction are: An immersed rod in water seems small and bent at the surface when it is observed from outside. A coin within a pot with water seems above from its original position. Twinkling of stars - The stars twinkle in the night sky because of the effects of atmospheric refraction. When starlight enters the atmosphere it is affected by winds in the atmosphere and by the regions with different temperatures and densities, therefore light rays bent (refracted) many times in random directions. Advance sunrise and delay in the sunset. The Rainbow (formed due to refraction, reflection, and dispersion.) The apparent position of the stars. Info Bits: Critical angle (θ_c) is the angle of incidence beyond which rays of light passing through a denser medium to the surface of a less dense medium are no longer refracted but totally reflected. Its value depends upon the nature of both mediums and color of rays. For critical angle light must pass from a denser medium to rarer medium. The complete reflection of light ray reaching an interface with a less dense medium when the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle is known as total internal reflection. Total internal reflection takes place only when a ray of light is travelling from denser to rarer medium and angle of incidence is greater than the critical angle, the ray is completely reflected from the surface. Looming in cold regions and Mirages in arid regions occur due to the optical illusions caused by the Total Internal reflection of light. Optical fibres, Endoscope function and sparkling of diamond works on the principle of total internal reflection.

questions.q: The colour of light that bends the most while passing through a prism is

It occurs due to the different velocities of different colours in a medium as the Refractive Index of the medium is different for the different colours. Violet light deviates the most and Red light deviates the least and hence, a pattern of light is obtained as VIBGYOR. Refraction is due to change in the speed of light as it enters from one transparent medium to another.

questions.explanation: When the air is cool and the ground is hot, mirages can appear. A layer of air slightly above the ground heats due to the heated ground. When the light moves through the cold air and into the layer of hot air it is refracted (bent). The light from the sky is practically bent into a U-shape by a layer of extremely warm air close to the ground.

questions.q: Due to which of the following phenomena, the coloured glass appears white when crushed into fine powder?
questions.explanation: Let's say the colour of the glass was "X". The colour of the light that an object scatters determines how it appears to the observer. When a coloured object is exposed to white light, it absorbs all colours but X (the colour of the object) and scatters the X light in all directions. Hence, we perceive the object's colour to be X. Similarly, When white light falls on an X-colored reflecting glass object on the ground, it partially reflects some of the white light and scatters some X light as well. The light that is reflected is entirely white; no other colours are absorbed by the glassy object. Therefore, we perceive a white object if the light reflected from the X-coloured glassy object is more white light than the scattered X-coloured light. Info Bits: Scattering is the phenomenon by which a beam of light is redirected in many different directions when it interacts with a particle of matter. Scattering occurs when a particle of light is fully absorbed and then emitted, while reflection is when a wave/particle is simply reflected off the surface without interacting. Light of shorter wavelength is scattered much more than the light of longer wavelength. Color of sky appears to blue due to the scattering of light. Note: In normal lighting conditions, photo receptor cells in human eye are most sensitive to colours between the wavelengths of 530-570 nanometer. The sensitivity of our eyes is most for yellowish-green colour. Persistence of vision is a phenomenon of the human eye wherein the image formed on the retina is retained by the brain for 1/16th of a second. Whereas, the impression of sound heard by our ears persist in our mind for 1/10th of a second. If another sound is heard before 1/10 second is over, the impression of the two sound mixes up and our mind cannot differentiate among the two.

questions.q: Which of the following is responsible for the production of a shadow?
questions.explanation: Shadows are formed because light travels in a straight line — this is called the rectilinear propagation of light. Rectilinear propagation of light: Light does not bend on its own; it travels in straight lines in a uniform medium. When an opaque object (like your hand or a book) comes in the path of light, it blocks the light rays from reaching a certain region behind the object. Shadow Formation: The region behind the object where light does not reach becomes dark — this dark area is what we see as a shadow. The shape of the shadow depends on the shape of the object blocking the light. Conditions needed for a shadow: A source of light (like the Sun or a torch) An opaque object (that blocks light) A screen or surface (like a wall or ground) where the shadow is seen

In other words, EM waves are composed of oscillating magnetic and electric fields. Examples: Light, Microwave, Infrared, Gamma rays. Info Bits: Properties of EM Waves: These are electrically neutral. Also called non-mechanical waves. Contains energy and Momentum Contain Photons Travel with a velocity of 3 x 10⁸ m/s - Alpha Rays, Canal Rays, Beta Rays and Cathode Rays are not EM waves as these waves contain charged ions and travel with a speed less than light. Note: Due to its very small wavelength, the light appears to travel in a straight line. This phenomenon is known as rectilinear propagation of light.

questions.q: Consider the following:
questions.explanation: Energy can be transferred from one place to another place through waves. Waves are of two types namely, Mechanical waves and Electromagnetic waves. Mechanical waves are produced due to vibrations in any state of the matter be it solid, liquid, gas or plasma. Some common examples of mechanical waves are waves formed in water, generated due to vibrations in liquid, sound waves generated due to the vibration in a gas (air). Mechanical waves travel through a medium in which energy gets transferred from one molecule to another molecule of the medium. Sound waves cannot travel in a vacuum as there is no medium or molecule through which they will travel from one place to another place. Electromagnetic waves: It is also called as EM waves. It is produced due to an accelerated electric charge and travels in space due to the periodic oscillation of electric as well as magnetic fields. These waves do not need a medium to travel; hence they can travel in vacuum, air or through any state of matter. The speed of the electromagnetic waves is equal to the speed of light i.e. 3 × 10⁸ m/s. Light is an electromechanical wave. James Clerk Maxwell (a Scottish scientist) explained Electromagnetic waves through his equations named Maxwell's equations theoretically. His theories were later demonstrated experimentally by Heinrich Hertz (a German physicist) while producing and receiving radio waves. He proved that the velocity of radio waves was the same as that of light. Electromagnetic Spectrum: In Submarine communication ELF (Extremely low frequency) of around 1 kHz Radio waves are used. Microwaves are used in Radars. From the given table we can compare the frequencies and the correct order of waves in ascending order will be → Submarine communication< AM radio<Shortwave radio<Radar. Hence, option B is correct.

questions.q: Which of the following radiations are used in mobile communication?
questions.explanation: Microwaves are used in mobile communication and Wi-Fi. Microwaves are radio waves of shorter wavelength and thus have a higher frequency, (v=c/λ, where v = frequency, c = speed of the light and λ = wavelength), Which is used to provide high speed wireless connections to transfer data from a source to the receiver. They are used for communication, cooking and in Radars. The wavelength of microwaves lies between a range of a few millimeters to tens of centimeters (1 mm to 0.1m).

questions.q: Which among the following principles is used by Bats?
questions.explanation: is used by Bats. They emit ultrasonic waves of high frequency (not audible to human ears) while they navigate to catch their prey. These high-frequency sound waves get reflected to the Bats as echoes upon hitting an obstacle and are sensed by the ears of the bat. This phenomenon is also known as echolocation as it helps in the calculation of the distance at which an obstacle is present.

questions.q: When sound travels in the form of the wave

options.A: The particles of the medium move from the source to the listener
options.B: Particles of the medium remain stationary
options.C: The particles of medium start vibrating up and down
options.D: The particles of the medium transfer energy without leaving their mean position
options.E: None or More

questions.explanation: As sound travels through a medium, its energy causes the molecules to move, creating an alternating compression and rarefaction pattern. Here particles of medium doesn't move with the sound wave. As the wave passes, the particles become energized and move from their original positions. After a particle passes its energy to nearby particles, the particle's motion diminishes until it is affected by another passing wave. Info Bits: Longitudinal waves are waves in which the particles of the medium oscillate along the direction of wave propagation. E.g. Sound waves, ultrasound waves, ocean waves, etc. Transverse waves are waves in which the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. E.g. Light, microwave, radio waves Sound can't travel in a vacuum Speed of sound in solid > liquid > gas The frequency of sound does not change with a change in medium. Pressure has no effect on the speed of sound.

questions.explanation: Oscillation is the to and fro movement of an object along its mean position. Frequency can be defined as the number of oscillations per second. It is expressed in hertz. We know that sound travels through the vibrations or oscillations produced in the particles of the medium. Pitch is a form of response produced by our ear in response to the sound wave entering our ear. The shrillness or pitch of a sound is determined by its frequency. Hence, if the frequency of vibration is higher i.e., no. of oscillations per second is higher the sound will have a higher pitch which means more sound waves are reaching our ear per unit time and if the frequency of vibration is lower the sound will have a lower pitch which means lesser number of sound waves is reaching our ear per unit time. As we know, the relationship between frequency, speed of light and wavelength is given by: (v=c/ λ, where v =frequency, c=speed of the light and λ =wavelength) Hence, the relationship between the wavelength of a sound wave and pitch of a sound can also be derived. Wavelength is the distance between two peaks (crests or troughs) of the waves. As the frequency is inversely proportional to the wavelength (from the above formula), A sound with a higher pitch will have a shorter wavelength and vice-versa. From the above formula, we can see that the frequency of a sound wave depends upon the speed of the light. As we know the speed of the light is constant so there will not be any variation in the frequency of a sound wave and consequently in the pitch of a sound wave due to the velocity. Hence, from the given options option A is the correct answer.

questions.q: We have two eyes because of the perception of the objects in the dimension of
questions.explanation: Our two eyes are positioned on the front of our heads, and it thus reduces our field of view in favour of what is called stereopsis. Shut one eye and the world looks flat – two-dimensional. Keep both eyes open and the world takes on the third dimension of depth. Because our eyes are separated by a few centimetres, each eye sees a slightly different image. Our brain combines the two images into one, using the extra information to tell us how close or far away things are.

questions.explanation: In physics, the quantities are classified as scalars and vectors. The difference is that a direction is associated with a vector but not with a scalar. A scalar quantity is a quantity with magnitude only. It is specified completely by a single number, along with the proper unit. Examples: Length, Volume, Speed, Mass, Density, Temperature, Pressure, Energy, Work, Power, time, etc. A vector quantity is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction and obeys the triangle law of addition or equivalently the parallelogram law of addition. Examples: Displacement, Velocity, Electric Field, Magnetic Field, Acceleration, etc. Info Bits: The Dot Product of two vectors gives scalar quantity, while the cross product gives vector Quantity. Torque is a vector quantity. Which one of the following is a scalar quantity? [65th BPSC] A. Force B. Pressure C. Velocity D. Acceleration E. None of the above/More than one of the above Ans. questions.q: If a feather, a rubber ball, and a wooden ball are falling freely simultaneously from the same height in a vacuum, then

options.B: The rubber ball will reach the ground first
options.C: The wooden ball will reach the ground first
options.D: All three will reach the ground together
options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: A free-falling object is an object which is falling under the influence of gravity only. In the case of free fall, the only force that acts on all objects is gravity. So, the three bodies falling freely will experience no air resistance acting and all three objects will accelerate at the same value of "g". Hence, all three objects will fall together.

questions.q: Two objects of different masses falling freely near the surface of the Moon would

options.B: undergo a change in their inertia
options.C: have same velocity at any instant

questions.explanation: The Question does not give the parameters for height and time. Assuming that both objects are dropped at the same time from the same reasonable height, they will have "The same velocity at any instant" as they fall freely near the surface of the moon. There are certain factors that affect the speed of an object as it falls. These include, mass, weight, force, velocity, acceleration and air resistance. When something falls, it falls because of gravity. Because that object feels a force, it accelerates, which means its velocity gets bigger and bigger as it falls. The strength with which the Earth pulls on something in the form of gravity is a type of acceleration. Earth pulls everything with the exact same amount. The force that objects feel may be different because they have different masses, but the acceleration on Earth they experience is exactly the same. Because Earth gives everything the exact same acceleration, objects with different masses will still hit the ground at the same time if they are dropped from the same height. However you might have noticed that if you drop a marble and a feather at the same time, the marble hits the floor first. That is not because of differences in the acceleration - which is constant on Earth, it is because air is pushing against the object in the opposite direction the Earth is pulling. This force is caused by air resistance. If two objects were dropped on the moon, where there is no air, they would fall at the same rate no matter how much they differ in mass.

questions.q: A second pendulum is mounted in a rocket; its period of oscillation decreases when the rocket

options.B: moves around the earth in a geostationary orbit
options.D: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Time Period of a second’s pendulum is given by, Time Period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$ where "L" is the effective length of the pendulum and "g" is the acceleration due to gravity. When a rocket moves upwards with uniform acceleration, the effective acceleration due to gravity increases, which tends to decrease the Time period of the Pendulum. On moving down with uniform acceleration effective acceleration due to gravity decreases hence time period increases. Info Bits: The time period of a Pendulum clock/Seconds Pendulum (Royal Pendulum) = 2 seconds Parameters that affect the time period of a simple pendulum are its length and the acceleration due to gravity. The period is completely independent of the mass of the bob.

questions.q: If the spinning speed of the earth increases, then the weight of the body at the equator will

options.E: None of the above/ more than one of the above

questions.explanation: The effect of rotation of the earth on acceleration due to gravity is given by the relation: $g_{\lambda} = g - R_e w^2 \cos^2\lambda$ where w is the angular velocity of the rotation of the earth and $\lambda$ is the angular position of that point. Now at the equator, $\lambda = 0^{\circ} => g_{\lambda} = g - R_e w^2$ So, as the spinning speed of earth increases, then 'g' at the equator decreases and thus the weight of the body decreases at the equator. It is important to note that at poles the value of $\lambda$ will be $90^{\circ}$. So, $g_{\lambda} = g$, Which means 'g' at the poles is independent of the rotation of the earth. Info Bits: Factor affecting the value of Acceleration due to Gravity - Variation of 'g' with Height: The value of g is inversely proportional to the height above the earth's surface. Therefore, it decreases with increasing height. - Variation of 'g' with Depth: The value of g is inversely proportional to the depth below the earth's surface but directly proportional to the mass of the earth. Therefore, the value of g decreases with increasing depth, but at the Centre of the earth, it becomes equal to zero. So, the value of g is maximum at the surface of the earth. - Variation of 'g' due to the shape of the Earth: The value of g at the equator is less than the value of g at the pole. - Variation of 'g' due to the Rotation of the Earth: The value of g decreases with the increase in the earth's rotation.

questions.q: For a body moving with constant speed in a horizontal circle, which of the following remains constant?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: If the speed of the object moving in circular motion is constant, it is uniform circular motion. And an object in uniform circular motion does experience acceleration, even though its speed is constant. For the object to move in a circle, the direction of its velocity must change constantly. This change in direction is what causes the acceleration, called centripetal acceleration and this centripetal acceleration vector is always pointed toward the centre of the circle. The kinetic energy depends only on the magnitude of the velocity and not on its direction. Since the speed (i.e. the magnitude of the velocity) is constant, the kinetic energy (½ mv²) remains constant.

questions.q: Centripetal force is responsible to

options.B: independent motion of the object in space
options.C: keeps the body moving along the circular path
options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: Centripetal force is responsible to keep the body moving along the circular path. Centripetal force: Centripetal force is directed towards the centre of the circular path (or which acts along the radius of the circle) and provides the necessary acceleration (v²/R) to the body of mass 'm' moving in a circular path of radius 'R' with a uniform speed 'v'. The value of Centripetal force is given by: $F_c = \frac{Mv^2}{R}$ Some examples of the Centripetal force: | Activity | Centripetal force | | --- | --- | | A stone tied to one end of a string rotating in a circle. | Centripetal force is provided by the string. | | Motion of Earth or any planet around the Sun. | Centripetal force is provided by the gravitational force exerted by the sun on the planet or the earth. | | A vehicle taking a circular turn on a horizontal road. | Friction force acts as the Centripetal force. |

questions.q: Satellite escape velocity is 11 km per second. If the satellite is launched at an angle of 60 degrees with the vertical, then the escape velocity will be

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: The minimum speed required for an object to reach infinity (i.e. escape from the earth) is called the escape speed, sometimes loosely called the escape velocity. The escape speed is given by the relation $v_{es} = \sqrt{(2GM / R)}$ where M is the mass of the earth and R is the radius of the earth. From the relation, it is clear that the escape speed is independent of the mass of the object and the angle with which it is thrown. On Earth, the escape speed is around 11.2km/s. If a body on earth is thrown with escape speed the path taken by it will be parabolic. If a body on earth is thrown with a speed greater than escape speed then the path taken by it will be hyperbolic. The minimum velocity needed by a satellite to escape from our planet is 11.2 km/s and that of moon is 2.3 km/s, which is about 5 times smaller than Earth.

So, for a fixed mass, when density increases, volume decreases, and when the volume increases, density decreases. Solids have the highest density, then liquids and gases have the least density. A light-year is the distance a beam of light travels in a single Earth year, which equates to approximately 6 trillion miles (9.7 trillion kilometers). Light is the fastest, and nothing travels faster than light. The speed of light in a vacuum, commonly denoted by ‘c’, is a universal physical constant and is precisely 671 million miles per hour. The nearest star to our sun, Proxima Centauri, is 4.2 light-years away, meaning the light we see from the star takes a little over four years to reach us. Astronomers also use Parsec as an alternative to the light-year. Short form for parallax-second, a parsec is equal to 3.26 light-years In the solar system, distance is measured in terms of the Astronomical Unit (AU). The AU is defined as the average distance between the Earth and the Sun. Parsec (pc) is the largest unit of distance which is used to measure astronomical objects. Note: Light takes 8 minutes and 20 sec to reach from the sun to Earth. Light takes 1.28 seconds to reach from the moon to Earth.

questions.explanation: The pitch of a vibrating string depends on its length, thickness, and the tension placed on the string. If two strings are the same length, the thicker string will have a lower pitch than the thinner string. If the length of this string is shortened, its pitch will increase. Hence, the answer is option E (A&D).

questions.q: Before playing the orchestra in a musical concert, a sitarist tries to adjust the tension and pluck the string suitably. By doing so, he/she is adjusting

options.B: frequency of the sitar string with the frequency of other musical instruments
options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: Before playing the orchestra in a musical concert, a Sitarist tries to adjust the tension and pluck the string suitably. By doing so, he/she is adjusting the frequency of the sitar string with the frequency of other musical instruments. The natural frequency in which a sitar will vibrate will depend upon the length of the string, diameter, density and tension produced in the string. A sitar string will have a fixed length, diameter and density, therefore the Sitarist tries to adjust the tension to tune the natural frequency of the guitar in line with other musical instruments in the orchestra. Hence, option B is the correct answer. Info Bits: Different kinds of melodious notes are generated by the Sitarist as he changes the effective length of the string by touching it at different points with his fingers. Thus the variation in amplitude or intensity of the sound is not decided by the tension in the string but it depends upon how the Sitarist is playing it. A loud or low note can be produced even from a taut string depending upon the wish and requirement of the Sitarist but he cannot change the natural frequency so he has to adjust the tension beforehand.

questions.q: Which of the following is equivalent to tesla?
questions.explanation: The tesla (symbol T) is the derived SI unit of magnetic flux density (also known as magnetic field strength, B), equivalent to one weber per square meter. It is a vector unit. Dimensionally, we have [B] = [F/qv] and the unit of B are Newton second / (coulomb metre). This unit is called tesla (T) named after Nikola Tesla (1856 – 1943). Tesla is a rather large unit. A smaller unit (non-SI) called *gauss (=10⁻⁴ tesla)* is also often used. The earth's magnetic field is about 3.6 × 10⁻⁵ T. The magnetic field is the area around a magnet in which there is magnetic force. Moving electric charges can make magnetic fields. Magnetic fields can be illustrated by magnetic flux lines. Info Bits: The system of units which is at present internationally accepted for measurement is the Système Internationale d' Unites (French for International System of Units), abbreviated as SI. The SI, with standard scheme of symbols, units and abbreviations, developed by the Bureau International des Poids et mesures (The International Bureau of Weights and Measures, BIPM) in 1971 were recently revised by the General Conference on Weights and Measures in November 2018. Magnetic force, which is the attraction or repulsion that results from the motion of electrically charged particles. It is the fundamental force behind phenomena like how electric motors work and how magnets are drawn to iron. Contact vs Non-Contact Forces | Type of Force | Examples | Key Features | | --- | --- | --- | | Contact Forces | Friction, Tension, Normal Force, Air Resistance, Applied Force | Forces that arise due to physical contact between two objects. | | Non-Contact Forces | Gravitational Force, Magnetic Force, Electrostatic Force | Forces that act at a distance without physical contact. |

questions.q: Which of the following is a non-contact force?
questions.explanation: A force exerted on an object by a body not in direct contact with it is known as a non-contact force. When two objects are not in physical contact with one another or when a force is exerted without any interaction, non-contact forces are at work. Non-contact forces are extremely rare in comparison to the various forms of contact forces.

questions.q: Current density is
questions.explanation: Current density (J) is a measure of the flow of electric current through a conductor per unit area. It is typically denoted in amperes per square meter (A/m²). The formula for current density is: J = I/A Current density is a vector quantity having both a direction and a scalar magnitude. The electric current flowing through a solid having units of charge per unit time is calculated towards the direction perpendicular to the flow of direction.
